title: Veteran Overmedication and Suicide Prevention Act of 2025
short_title: Veteran Overmedication and Suicide Prevention Act of 2025
plain_summary: This law tells the Department of Veterans Affairs to hire the National Academies to study veteran suicides from the past five years. The study must look at veterans who got medical care from the VA during the five years before they died. It will count how many veterans died by suicide, violent death, or accidental death and collect information about these veterans. The study will also look at what medications and treatments these veterans received and report the results to Congress and the public.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Veteran Overmedication and Suicide Prevention Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Department of Veterans Affairs Independent Review of Certain Deaths of Veterans by Suicide
sections.text: The Secretary of Veterans Affairs must ask the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine to study veteran suicides. This study must happen within ninety days after this law is passed. The study will look at all veterans who died by suicide in the five years before this law was passed. These veterans must have received medical care from the Department of Veterans Affairs during the five years before they died. The study will count how many veterans died by suicide, violent deaths, and accidental deaths during this five year period. It will describe each veteran by age, gender, race, and ethnicity. The study will make a complete list of all prescription drugs and other substances found in the bodies of these veterans when they died. It will specifically note any medications that had a black box warning, which means a warning about serious or life threatening risks. It will also note medications used for purposes other than what they were originally approved for, mind altering medications, and medications that warn about thoughts of suicide. The study will summarize the medical conditions that led doctors to prescribe these medications. This includes post traumatic stress disorder, traumatic brain injury, military sexual trauma, and other anxiety and depression disorders. The study will count how many veterans were taking multiple medications at the same time for these conditions. It will also count how many veterans were not taking any medications prescribed by Veterans Affairs doctors. The study will compare how many veterans received counseling or therapy first versus how many received only medication for their mental health conditions. It will count cases where counseling or therapy was tried first but did not work, leading to medication being prescribed. The study will describe how the Department decides what medications to prescribe and how they update these guidelines. It will analyze how the Department measures pain scores during medical visits and whether this relates to veterans taking multiple medications at once. The study will describe the Department's efforts to hire enough mental health professionals like counselors and therapists. This includes any problems with hiring and training these professionals and plans to fix these problems. It will review requirements for these jobs and compare them to other mental health jobs in the Department. The study will look at training programs and any barriers to participation. It will describe how the Department finds gaps in mental health staffing and determines the right number of professionals needed. The study will describe efforts to create job categories for mental health counselors and marriage and family therapists. It will also describe efforts to make sure professional boards for these jobs include only people from these professions. The study will identify the percentage of veterans who had combat experience or trauma related to combat. It will identify Veterans Affairs medical facilities with unusually high rates of prescriptions and suicides. The study will analyze how the Department works with state Medicaid programs and Medicare. This includes sharing prescription and mental health information about veterans. It will check whether Department staff look at state prescription monitoring programs before giving medications to veterans. It will describe how the Department works with doctors outside the Veterans Affairs system to prevent giving veterans too many medications. It will describe what the Department does when they find a veteran is taking too many medications. The study will analyze how Veterans Affairs medical centers work with medical examiners to determine how and why veterans died. It will identify the best way to collect and share veteran death certificate information between the Department of Veterans Affairs, the Department of Defense, states, and tribal groups. It will describe how the Department collects and reports information from veteran death certificates. The study will assess any patterns the National Academies finds in this information. The study will make recommendations to improve veteran safety and wellbeing. The Secretary must make sure all this information can be analyzed together to help update treatment guidelines. Information about veterans who died by suicide, violent deaths, and accidental deaths must be kept separate and organized by year. The National Academies must finish this study and give a report to the Secretary within one hundred eighty days of starting the work. The Secretary must give this report to Congress within thirty days of receiving it and make it available to the public. In this section, black box warning means a warning on prescription drug labels about serious or life threatening risks. Covered veteran means a veteran who received hospital care or medical services from the Department of Veterans Affairs during the five years before their death. First line treatment means a potential treatment that has been evaluated and given a high score in treatment guidelines. State means all states, territories, possessions of the United States, the District of Columbia, and Puerto Rico.
